title: Experimental administrative population census | Stats NZ
content:
## Experimental administrative population census
This is our experimental section. The administrative population census is an experimental output using a different methodology than what is currently used to produce the census or the official estimated resident population . These are not official statistics, and we advise caution when using the data.
7 November 2023: We have updated this page to include information about households and relationships. This extends the APC (third iteration) we published in October 2023.
The administrative population census (APC) derives census-type information from linked administrative data in the Integrated Data Infrastructure (IDI). The APC is part of Stats NZ’s census transformation programme , which explores the use of administrative data to produce census information.

## Third iteration of the APC, October 2023
This third iteration of the APC provides:
- an annual time series from 2006 to 2022, from underlying longitudinal unit record data
- estimates of the New Zealand resident population
- the following variables about individuals: population structure: age, sex, number of children born location: geography: RC, TALB, SA2, SA3, meshblock, address ID; population mobility: address 1 year ago, address 5 years ago, and years at usual residence ethnicity, culture, and identity: ethnicity levels 1 to 4, Māori descent, birthplace, years since arrival in New Zealand income: total personal income by income source education and training: highest qualification, field of study, study participation work: employment indicator, employment status, industry, sector of ownership
- population structure: age, sex, number of children born
- location: geography: RC, TALB, SA2, SA3, meshblock, address ID; population mobility: address 1 year ago, address 5 years ago, and years at usual residence
- ethnicity, culture, and identity: ethnicity levels 1 to 4, Māori descent, birthplace, years since arrival in New Zealand
- income: total personal income by income source
- education and training: highest qualification, field of study, study participation
- work: employment indicator, employment status, industry, sector of ownership
- estimates of the households in New Zealand
- the following variables about households: household size: number of usual residents in a household, number of usual residents under 15 years old, number of usual residents aged 15 and over, age of youngest person household income: total household income, total household income by income source relationships: between individuals.
- household size: number of usual residents in a household, number of usual residents under 15 years old, number of usual residents aged 15 and over, age of youngest person
- household income: total household income, total household income by income source
- relationships: between individuals.
A first iteration of the APC was released in August 2021 and a second iteration in October 2022.
There are further APC releases planned in the future, with extensions, changes, and other improvements based on the feedback received and feasibility.
- Experimental administrative population census (third iteration), totals by topic, 2006–2022 Excel spreadsheet, 517 KB

Excel spreadsheet, 517 KB
- Experimental administrative population census (third iteration), household information, totals by topic, 2006–2021 Excel spreadsheet, 304 KB

Excel spreadsheet, 304 KB
See Experimental administrative population census (third iteration) – data files for more detailed, machine-readable data files. The interactive tool below provides the ability to download customised tables.
APC unit record data is available to all IDI researchers as part of the standard tables.

## Data sources and methods
Experimental administrative population census (third iteration): Data sources, methods, and quality for household information describes the data sources and general approach to the methods used to compile the administrative-based household information. It provides an overall summary of the quality of household estimates, variables, relationships, and household usual residence address. The first iteration of household information covers the period 2006 to 2021.
Experimental administrative population census (third iteration): Changes to data, methods, and quality summarises changes made in the third iteration of the APC individual population and variables.
Experimental administrative population census: Data sources, methods, and quality (second iteration) describes the data sources and general approach to the methods used to compile the administrative population census, and provides an overall summary of the quality of the APC. The second iteration covers the period 2006–2021.
Experimental administrative population census (third iteration): Information by variable provides detail about the data sources, methods and quality measures for the resident population, and for each of the variables in the APC.

## Information for Māori about the APC
Information for Māori about the experimental administrative population census brings together information that is most directly relevant to iwi-Māori to support their use of the data and involvement in the development of the APC.

## The APC is different to a survey-based census
The APC is a different way of producing census information because it uses administrative data as the main data source, rather than asking everyone to fill out a questionnaire. Administrative data includes data such as birth registrations and tax records which are already collected by government agencies.
The APC release in 2023 builds on Experimental population estimates from linked administrative data released in 2016, 2017, and 2018, extensive and detailed research on the quality of administrative data for census variables, and the first and second APC iterations released in 2021 and 2022.
The use of administrative data means the APC is not constrained by the same limitations as a traditional census reliant on surveying the whole population. For example, results can be compiled annually and information for some variables is more detailed than can be obtained from a census questionnaire. The underlying unit-record data is inherently longitudinal and provides a record of events such as moving to a new address, qualifications gained, and birth of a child. Ethnicity and usual residence are reported by individuals to one or more agencies at different times, and in different contexts. By combining information from multiple agencies we can improve quality compared with what can be achieved within a single agency. Improvements in agency collection practices and in the methods for combining multiple sources would further increase quality.
The APC population count is almost always higher than the corresponding census counts, but there is a small net undercount of the total population compared with official population estimates. Work on estimation models to adjust for undercoverage and overcoverage of the APC population is in progress. However, there currently is no coverage adjustment made to the APC.
Overall, results for this third iteration of the APC show good agreement with the expected population patterns and distributions of census variables at high levels of aggregation. Many APC variables are derived from a formal record of transactions or events captured in administrative systems such as tax, education, and birth registrations, and the information aligns closely with the statistical concepts we seek to measure. There is still a need for additional data sources to improve coverage for some groups. New Zealand administrative systems have limited information for new migrants (for example, Māori descent) and for events that occur overseas (for example, highest qualifications attained, children born). Statistical imputation for remaining missing values is planned to be included in future iterations to improve the representativeness of the administrative data.
Administrative data of suitable quality is not available for all census variables. For example, iwi affiliation data is a critical requirement for iwi-Māori, but currently there is a lack of suitable alternative sources. As a key population indicator, alternative sources for iwi affiliation would need to provide high coverage for those of Māori descent. For some other census topics that have limited information from administrative data, such as language spoken and activity limitations, a sample survey may be required as part of any future census based largely on administrative data.
The target population of households in the APC is usually occupied private dwellings. The same target population as the official household estimates. This is different from the target population for census households, which includes only dwellings occupied on census night, and excludes dwellings where people are away on census night.   Admin-derived households are one or more individuals who share the same usual residence address, with addresses serving as a proxy for dwellings. The APC household count is very close to the census counts, but is lower compared with official household estimates.
The quality of household variables and household membership are inextricably intertwined. Small inaccuracies in household membership from admin and census data quickly compound. We have derived a household comparability measure to identify census households that can most reasonably be used to assess the quality of admin-derived households. Of the highly comparable households, we estimate that 72 percent of admin-derived households have the exact same household membership as reported in the 2018 Census. For household variables, the agreement rate with census ranges from 69 to 93 percent.
Relationships between individuals are included in the first release of the admin-derived household data as a step towards forming family information in the next iteration. The scope is familial relationships between any two individuals in the APC resident population. This scope is larger than census, which limits relationships to those between individuals living in the same household. This opens opportunities for researchers to create networks of related people and support networks outside the household. Overall, 89 percent of 2018 Census familial relationships are found in admin-derived familial relationships, 99 percent of which agree with the census relationship type.
While there is more work to do, the APC already demonstrates the considerable potential of administrative data to provide information about the New Zealand population on an annual basis, underpinned by the power of longitudinal unit record data. We look forward to hearing your feedback as we continue to develop the APC.

## We need your feedback
The experimental APC has been released to show what can be achieved currently with administrative data, and to provide data users the opportunity to be involved in further development.
We’re seeking feedback from everyone, including government agencies, local government, iwi and Māori organisations, academics, and the general public.
To give feedback or for further information: email censustransformation@stats.govt.nz
We are especially interested in your views on:
- How well the data meets your information needs, including the impact of differences compared with the traditional census.
- The data sources and methods used to derive specific variables in the APC, and any suggestions for improvements. See Experimental administrative population census (third iteration): Information by variable for details.
- What you would like to see in the next iteration of the APC and why.
